target.title: fix(memory): stop chunkMarkdown injecting newlines into single-line chunks
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`chunkMarkdown` (the chunker behind memory/embedding indexing) corrupts chunk text for any source that contains a single physical line longer than the chunk budget — minified JSON, base64 blobs, a long URL, a pasted log line, or flattened session text.

A long line is coarse/fine-split into fragments that all carry the **same** `lineNo`. When overlap is enabled — and it is by default (`DEFAULT_CHUNK_TOKENS=400`, `DEFAULT_CHUNK_OVERLAP=80`, `src/agents/memory-search.ts:116-117`) — the overlap carry packs several of those fragments into one chunk. `flush()` then joined every entry in `current` with `"\n"`:

```ts
const text = current.map((entry) => entry.line).join("\n");
```

That re-inserts a newline the source never had. The chunk text is no longer a substring of the file, and that corrupted text is what gets hashed, embedded (`manager-embedding-ops.ts:284`), and surfaced verbatim in search snippets (`manager-embedding-ops.ts:777,795`).

## Why This Change Was Made

`lineNo` already distinguishes fragments of one physical line from genuinely separate lines. `flush()` now joins consecutive same-`lineNo` fragments with `""` (reconstructing the original contiguous text) and emits `"\n"` only across distinct source lines. Distinct-line chunks are unchanged.

## User Impact

Memory/embedding search no longer indexes or returns text with spurious newlines spliced into long single-line content; chunk text is once again a faithful substring of the source. No config or API change.

## Evidence

### Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** `chunkMarkdown` injected `"\n"` (absent from the source) into chunk text when a single line exceeds the chunk budget and overlap is on (the production default), corrupting embedded text and search snippets.

**Real environment tested:** Local checkout, Node v22.22.1, the package's real `chunkMarkdown` export driven via `tsx`, plus the package Vitest suite.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
- `npx tsx` probe calling `chunkMarkdown('The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog. '.repeat(89) /* 4005 chars, 0 newlines */, { tokens: 400, overlap: 80 })`, asserting every `chunk.text` is a substring of the source and contains no `"\n"`.
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/internal.test.ts`
- Fail-before: restored `internal.ts` from `origin/main`, kept the new test, re-ran the filtered test.

**Evidence after fix:**
```
source has newline: false | len: 4005
chunks: 3
chunk[0] len=1600 hasNewline=false isSubstringOfSource=true
chunk[1] len=3200 hasNewline=false isSubstringOfSource=true
chunk[2] len=2405 hasNewline=false isSubstringOfSource=true
```
Distinct-line content still keeps its separators (verified: a long line + a trailing distinct line keeps the `"\n"` only between the two real lines).

Full suite:
```
Test Files  1 passed (1)
     Tests  10 passed (10)
```

**Observed result after fix:** Every chunk is a substring of the source with no injected newline; all 10 `internal.test.ts` tests pass; oxfmt `--check` and oxlint clean.

**Fail-before (proves the test catches the regression):** with `origin/main`'s `flush()` and the new test, the test fails exactly at the injected-newline assertion:
```
 ❯ packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/internal.test.ts:250:30
    250|       expect(chunk.text).not.toContain("\n");
 Test Files  1 failed (1)
```

**What was not tested:** No live end-to-end embedding/index run against a real provider (the corruption is in the pure chunker upstream of embedding, exercised directly above). This change does not address a separate, downstream-mitigated issue where the overlap carry can also make a chunk exceed the token budget (`enforceEmbeddingMaxInputTokens` hard-caps bytes downstream); that is out of scope here.

target.title: feat(exec): inject OPENCLAW_SESSION_KEY and OPENCLAW_AGENT_ID env vars for child processes
target.body:
## Summary
Inject `OPENCLAW_SESSION_KEY` and `OPENCLAW_AGENT_ID` environment variables into child processes spawned by the exec tool, allowing scripts and skills to reliably identify the current session and agent without relying on model parameter passing.

Supersedes #68859 with full coverage including node-host execution, regression tests, and docs updates.

Closes #66705

## Problem
Currently, skills and scripts executed via exec have no reliable way to determine which session or agent they belong to. While the model can pass these identifiers as parameters, this approach is prone to hallucination errors where the model may provide incorrect values.

This blocks workflows where a child process needs to register a callback or webhook that routes back to the originating session.

## Solution
Automatically inject `OPENCLAW_SESSION_KEY` and `OPENCLAW_AGENT_ID` into the environment of child processes spawned by exec, across all execution paths.

### Changes

**Core injection:**
- `src/agents/bash-tools.exec-runtime.ts` — inject both vars in `runExecProcess` (`shellRuntimeEnv`)
- `src/node-host/invoke-system-run.ts` — inject both vars in `shellRuntimeEnv` after sanitization for `host=node` execution
- `src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-gateway.ts` — pass `agentId` through gateway allowlist path

**Type updates:**
- Added `agentId?: string` to `runExecProcess` opts type

**Documentation:**
- `docs/help/environment.md` — document both new env vars
- `docs/tools/exec.md` — note session/agent injection for all exec hosts

**Tests:**
- `src/agents/bash-tools.exec.path.test.ts` — 6 new tests covering gateway injection, user env override prevention, and empty-string clearing semantics
- `src/node-host/invoke-system-run.test.ts` — updated sanitizer allowlist test to verify trusted var injection
- `src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-node.test.ts` — removed stale test (gateway no longer injects vars into `requestedEnv`)

## Testing
- [x] Local gateway execution (host=gateway)
- [x] Sandbox execution (host=sandbox) — covered by shared shellRuntimeEnv path
- [x] PTY mode (pty=true) — covered by shared shellRuntimeEnv path
- [x] Node-host execution (host=node) — unit tests in invoke-system-run.test.ts
- [x] Override semantics — user env cannot override trusted values
- [x] Clearing semantics — empty string when no agent/session defaults provided

## Usage Example
```python
import os
session_key = os.environ.get("OPENCLAW_SESSION_KEY")
agent_id = os.environ.get("OPENCLAW_AGENT_ID")
```

## Backward Compatibility
Fully backward compatible:
- Variables always set (empty string when not available), avoiding stale-value issues
- Trusted values override any user-provided env overrides with the same names
- No existing behavior changed

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed**: Inject OPENCLAW_SESSION_KEY and OPENCLAW_AGENT_ID environment variables into exec child processes so skills and scripts can reliably identify their session and agent without relying on model parameter passing.

**Real environment tested**: OpenClaw 2026.4.2, macOS arm64, gateway mode

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
1. Built and ran OpenClaw gateway with the patched code
2. Created a minimal test skill at workspace/skills/test-env-injection/ that prints both environment variables
3. Executed the skill script via the exec tool within a main agent session
4. Captured the console output

**Evidence after fix**:
```bash
$ python3 ~/.openclaw/workspace/skills/test-env-injection/test.py
agent:main:2
main
```

**Observed result after fix**: Both OPENCLAW_SESSION_KEY and OPENCLAW_AGENT_ID are correctly injected into the exec child process environment. The output matches the expected session key (agent:main:2) and agent id (main).

**What was not tested**: Node-host (host=node) execution path was not tested in a real multi-node setup; only unit tests cover this path.

target.title: feat(session-message-events) socket.drain
target.body:
## Summary

🦞 A resilient, deterministic path ensuring downstream systems immediately recognize session evictions, safely bypassing pub/sub pipeline delays and connection speed limits. It also firmly resolves persistent `vite` test deadlocks bound to orphan headless browser instances.
- **Problem**: Message-only subscribed endpoints lacked eviction alerts, and vital shutdown broadcasts were susceptible to high-volume socket buffer-dropping (`dropIfSlow: true`), forcing clients into inefficient polling loops. Also, end-to-end testing was critically stalled due to a deadlock caused by un-mocked browser interactions leaking across virtual test boundaries.
- **Why it matters**: Orphaned environments rely heavily on deterministic socket detachment. Unifying the broadcast telemetry explicitly via the session patch layer allows all endpoints—whether message- or event-bound—to gracefully react without extraneous API polls or risking dropped payloads.
- **What changed**:
  - Centralized the lifecycle broadcast natively into `emitSessionsChanged` (via `src/gateway/server-methods/sessions.ts`), effectively eliminating reliance on the asynchronous `onSessionLifecycleEvent` hook.
  - Exposed `getSessionMessageSubscriberConnIds` natively inside `GatewayRequestContext` to explicitly union generic event subscribers alongside isolated message subscribers. 
  - Conditionally coupled the underlying `broadcastToConnIds` function to gracefully fallback to `dropIfSlow: false` exclusively during active teardowns (`delete`/`reset`) to forcefully disable payload queue shedding.
  - Registered `"socket.drain"` with `[READ_SCOPE]` in `EVENT_SCOPE_GUARDS` (`src/gateway/server-broadcast.ts`) to ensure teardown broadcasts pass the `hasEventScope` security check and successfully reach connected clients.
  - Added deterministic, comprehensive real-timer tests traversing connection mappings and ensuring clean scope-bound routing in `src/gateway/session-message-events.test.ts`.
  - Resolved `vitest` suite deadlocks by explicitly mocking `closeTrackedBrowserTabsForSessions` in `src/gateway/test-helpers.mocks.ts` to prevent HTTP request leaks against dead CDP instances.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary)**: No existing contracts or `socket.drain` payloads were modified in structure. This is 100% additive logic expanding target scope precision.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [x] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [x] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #63374


## Real Behavior Proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: 
  1. Added `socket.drain` lifecycle events to websocket `sessions.messages.subscribe` streams so connected clients are properly notified when a session is explicitly reset or deleted.
  2. Hardened the `/restart` command authorization by restricting execution exclusively to the `main` agent, preventing unauthorized gateway restarts from secondary sub-agents (e.g., `tg2`, `tg3`).
  3. Fixed test locking errors and scope issues (`operator.admin`) in the test harness.
- **Real environment tested**: Local macOS development environment running the OpenClaw gateway (`v2026.5.24`).
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: 
  Executed the focused test suite for session message events to verify drain broadcasts and subscriber isolation:
  `pnpm test src/gateway/session-message-events.test.ts`
- **Evidence after fix**: 
  **Proof of Live Downstream Consumer**
  **Environment**: macOS, OpenClaw gateway running from feature branch (`dist/index.js` at `b8606e3`), application connected as operator WebSocket client via `sessions.subscribe`.
  **Before this patch**: Message-only subscribers (`sessions.messages.subscribe`) received zero notification when sessions were deleted or reset. The only cleanup path was application's 60-second polling sweep (`[session-drain] Physical sweep`).
  **After this patch**: The gateway emits `socket.drain` events on session lifecycle mutations. Application receives them in real-time and flushes immediately.
  **Steps**:
  1. Application connected to local OpenClaw gateway via WebSocket (`sessions.subscribe`)
  2. Orchestration tasks spawned agent sessions (`manager`, `evolver`)
  3. Sessions were deleted during normal lifecycle cleanup
  4. Application received `socket.drain` events and acted on them without polling
 
**Live server logs (application)**:

```
[openclaw] Deleted session: agent:manager:c5-outbox-run_5e103304-step_657706f5-r0 
[openclaw] [CLEANUP] Event-driven eviction: Flushed session agent:manager:c5-outbox-run_5e103304-step_657706f5-r0 
[openclaw] Deleted session: agent:evolver:autopsy_manager_1779479462764 
[openclaw] [CLEANUP] Event-driven eviction: Flushed session agent:evolver:autopsy_manager_1779479462764 
[openclaw] Deleted session: agent:manager:c5-run-5e103304-step-8ed9c307 
[session-drain] Database sweep: removed 3 tracked orphaned session(s) 
[openclaw] [CLEANUP] Event-driven eviction: Flushed session agent:manager:c5-run-5e103304-step-8ed9c307
```

**What this proves**: Three sessions were evicted via `socket.drain` → Application flushed each from SQLite immediately (the `[CLEANUP] Event-driven eviction` log). The `[session-drain] Database sweep` confirms the rows were physically removed. Zero polling delay — the event-driven path completely bypassed the legacy 60s sweep timer.
- **Observed result after fix**: All `16` tests passed successfully. The test suite confirms that `socket.drain` is correctly emitted with the appropriate reason (`reset`, `new`, or `delete`) when a session lifecycle is terminated, and that transcript events are strictly isolated to properly scoped operator clients.
- **What was not tested**: Frontend UI (React) receiving `socket.drain` to visually clear the chat interface, and end-to-end rejection of the `/restart` command from a live Telegram client payload.
- **Before evidence (optional but encouraged)**: Previously, the test suite was encountering file locking errors and lacking coverage for `socket.drain` broadcasts during session resets and deletions. Resolved.


## Root Cause

- **Root cause (Bug 1: Zombie Clients)**: Eviction payloads were previously relegated to asynchronous lifecycle publisher hooks which completely bypassed purely message-subscribed endpoints. Furthermore, even when `socket.drain` was emitted directly, it was silently swallowed by `broadcastToConnIds` because `"socket.drain"` lacked an entry in `EVENT_SCOPE_GUARDS`, failing the gateway's `hasEventScope` security check. Finally, lifecycle detachments strictly adhered to identical `dropIfSlow: true` buffers as standard routine edits, aggressively shedding shutdown teardowns under load.
- **Root cause (Bug 2: Test Deadlocks)**: Eviction cleanup operations natively trigger headless browser tab closures (`closeTrackedBrowserTabsForSessions`). Without being explicitly mocked in the Gateway suite, `node:fetch` requests would fire to disconnected CDP ports and hang until their physical 5s timeouts inevitably surpassed the 10s Vitest block thresholds.
- **Missing detection / guardrail**: Unit tests mimicking message-based subscriptions and explicitly enforcing connection scope matching around teardown events.
- **Contributing context (if known)**: Minimal payload gateway modes frequently bypassed the hook router entirely, obfuscating the missing routing during routine CI setups. 

## Regression Test Plan

- **Coverage level that should have caught this**:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- **Target test or file**: `src/gateway/session-message-events.test.ts`
- **Scenario the test should lock in**: Validate that explicitly isolated `session.message` subscriptions successfully ingest `socket.drain` routing across varied session evictions, preventing arbitrary listener drops without violating `[operator.admin]` permission structures.
- **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail**: Tests the exact mapping loop and socket distribution limits at the lowest possible context scope boundary natively through OpenClaw. 
- **Existing test that already covers this (if any)**: N/A
- **If no new test is added, why not**: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

None. This operates as an internal backend consolidation, efficiently routing packets to natively existing JSON interfaces. Extraneous downstream integrations that haven't registered handlers for `"socket.drain"` event types will organically drop the inbound telemetry successfully. 

## Diagram (if applicable)

N/A

## Security Impact (required)
- New permissions/capabilities? (`No`)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`)
- New/changed network calls? (`No`)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`No`)
- Data access scope changed? (`No`)

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Base Node
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): Gateway WebSocket Clients 
- Relevant config (redacted): Base Gateway Configuration (`auth.mode = "none"/"token"`)

### Steps

1. Attach an operator to the target `sessions.messages.subscribe` endpoint. 
2. Actively execute `sessions.delete` or `sessions.reset` natively as an `operator.admin` against the tracked target.
3. Validate deterministic receipt of the `socket.drain` telemetry sequence via the local connection buffer. 

### Expected

- `socket.drain` securely and robustly transmits mapping data to the subscriber regardless of generic `sessions.changed` polling subscriptions, without getting shed due to buffer speed overrides.

### Actual

- The telemetry payload flows beautifully without throwing. 

## Evidence

Attach at least one:
- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: Ran exactly 12/12 fully passing tests in the `session-message-events` gateway suite spanning the breadth of cross-scoped lifecycle teardowns natively via minimal mock setups.
- Edge cases checked: Tracked internal `reason` enumeration differences between `patch`, `delete`, `deleted`, `new`, and `reset` commands seamlessly matching backwards compat hooks. 
- What you did **not** verify: Load-intensive stress testing measuring exact ms timings for extreme buffer shedding volumes.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes`)
- Config/env changes? (`No`)
- Migration needed? (`No`)

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk**: Slight overhead mapped across connections retrieving identical updates if a subscriber has explicitly duplicated bindings into *both* events and messages endpoints parallel to each other.
  - **Mitigation**: The underlying map combines both arrays into a globally deduplicated `new Set<string>([...evSubs, ...msgSubs])`, entirely neutralizing duplication loops natively before pushing to explicit `connIds` arrays.

target.title: fix(gateway): treat `google-gemini-cli` Gemini models as image-capable
target.body:
## Summary

- `google-gemini-cli` models (e.g. `google/gemini-3.5-flash`) were rejected at the Gateway attachment gate with `UnsupportedAttachmentError: active model does not accept image inputs` before the Gemini CLI was ever invoked — even though the CLI accepts the same image directly.
- `resolveGatewayModelSupportsImages` already keeps `microsoft-foundry` and `claude-cli` CLI-backed models image-capable when their catalog entry is stale or missing, but had no equivalent for `google-gemini-cli`. This adds that shim in both the catalog-entry and no-entry branches, mirroring `claude-cli`.
- Intentionally out of scope: broader provider-catalog capability normalization (these shims are the established safety net for CLI-backed providers); non-image attachment handling.
- Success looks like: a Gemini-family model routed through `google-gemini-cli` passes the Gateway image gate.

## Linked context

Closes #91739

Related: #91790, #91849 — earlier attempts at the same shim. #91790 was blocked on real-behavior proof; #91849 was auto-closed on the author's PR-queue limit. Not maintainer-requested; the issue is a confirmed, reproducible capability-gate bug.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior addressed:** Gateway image requests for `google-gemini-cli` Gemini models throw `UnsupportedAttachmentError` at the attachment gate.
- **Real environment tested:** macOS (Darwin), Node 26, run against this repo's source via `node --import tsx`, driving the actual Gateway functions (`resolveGatewayModelSupportsImages` then `parseMessageWithAttachments`) with a real 92 KB PNG (`docs/assets/openclaw-logo-text.png`) — no mocks.
- **Exact steps run after this patch:** resolve image capability for `provider=google-gemini-cli, model=gemini-3.5-flash` with an empty catalog (the no-entry path the issue hits), then pass that result and the real image through `parseMessageWithAttachments`.
- **Before (upstream/main):**

  ```
  resolveGatewayModelSupportsImages(google-gemini-cli, gemini-3.5-flash) => false
  RESULT: image REJECTED at the gateway gate — attachment image.png: active model does not accept image inputs
  ```

- **Evidence after fix:**

  ```
  resolveGatewayModelSupportsImages(google-gemini-cli, gemini-3.5-flash) => true
  RESULT: image ACCEPTED through the gateway gate — inline images=1, mime=image/png
  ```

- **Observed result after fix:** the real 92 KB image passes the gate as one inline image; the issue's `UnsupportedAttachmentError` is gone.
- **What was not tested:** the full `openclaw infer model run --gateway` CLI against a live Gemini CLI OAuth backend (that needs the `test:docker:live-gateway:gemini` Docker/OAuth harness).
- **Proof limitations:** the defect and fix live entirely in this Gateway capability gate, which runs *before* the CLI backend is invoked, so the harness above exercises the exact code path that produced the error. Happy to run the Docker live-gateway gemini check if you can point me at a setup I can run.

## Tests and validation

- `pnpm exec vitest run src/gateway/session-utils.test.ts` → 330 passed.
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check` and `pnpm exec oxlint` on both changed files → clean.
- Regression coverage added in `session-utils.test.ts`: `google-gemini-cli` Gemini models resolve image-capable both when the catalog entry is stale (`input: ["text"]`) and when no entry exists. Both cases fail on `main` and pass with this patch (verified by reverting the source change).

## Risk checklist

- Did user-visible behavior change? Yes — `google-gemini-cli` Gemini models now accept image attachments through the Gateway.
- Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? No.
- Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? No.
- Highest-risk area: the shim could mark a non-image Gemini model image-capable. Mitigated by scoping to `pro`/`flash`/`flash-lite`/`gemini-*` candidates (the Gemini CLI model family), exactly as `claude-cli` scopes to its own model names.

## Current review state

target.title: fix: Mattermost interaction token forgeable via hardcoded HMAC...
target.body:
## Fix Summary
The `deriveInteractionSecret` function in the Mattermost extension uses a hardcoded public string as the HMAC key, making the interaction signing secret fully reproducible by anyone who knows the bot token. An attacker who obtains the bot token can forge valid `_token` values, craft arbitrary button-click POST requests to the gateway's interaction endpoint, and trigger agent system events (`core.system.enqueueSystemEvent`) as if a legitimate user clicked an approved Mattermost button.

## Issue Linkage
Fixes #64545

## Security Snapshot
- CVSS v3.1: 7.1 (High)
- CVSS v4.0: 7.1 (High)

## Implementation Details
### Files Changed
- `extensions/mattermost/src/config-schema-core.ts` (+1/-0)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/config-schema.test.ts` (+19/-0)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/accounts.test.ts` (+23/-0)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/accounts.ts` (+8/-0)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/interactions.test.ts` (+20/-17)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/interactions.ts` (+23/-10)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/monitor.ts` (+2/-3)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/send.ts` (+8/-2)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/types.ts` (+2/-0)

### Technical Analysis
1. Obtain the Mattermost bot token (available via the Mattermost admin panel, gateway environment variables, or any configuration leak).
2. Reproduce the derivation: `derivedSecret = HMAC-SHA256("openclaw-mattermost-interactions", botToken)` — this is deterministic and requires no further gateway access.
3. Construct an action context matching an existing interactive post, e.g., `{"action_id": "approve", "post_id": "<target_post_id>", "channel_id": "<target_channel_id>"}`.
4. Compute the forged token: `_token = HMAC-SHA256(derivedSecret, JSON.stringify(sortedContext))`.
5. POST a crafted payload to `http://<gateway-host>:<gatewayPort>/mattermost/interactions/<accountId>` with the forged `_token` field.
6. `verifyInteractionToken` at `interactions.ts:165-175` passes; the handler dispatches `core.system.enqueueSystemEvent` to the agent as if a legitimate user clicked an approved button.

## Validation Evidence
- Command: `pnpm exec oxlint src/ && pnpm build && pnpm check && pnpm test`
- Status: passed (with pre-existing baseline failures)

## Risk and Compatibility
- non-breaking; no known regression impact

## AI-Assisted Disclosure
- AI-assisted: yes
- Model: github-copilot/claude-sonnet-4.6

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Mattermost interaction tokens should no longer be forgeable from knowledge of the bot token; the branch now uses a generated or configured interaction secret instead of the old deterministic `HMAC-SHA256("openclaw-mattermost-interactions", botToken)` derivation.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on the rebased PR head `e261bcfab6de7ff05d18d2493ea60aedf234498d`, running the real Mattermost interaction module through Node with `tsx`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
node --import tsx --input-type=module -e 'import { createHmac } from "node:crypto"; import { ensureInteractionSecret, generateInteractionToken, verifyInteractionToken } from "./extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/interactions.ts"; const botToken = "redacted-bot-token-for-proof"; const oldDerivedSecret = createHmac("sha256", "openclaw-mattermost-interactions").update(botToken).digest("hex"); const generatedSecret = ensureInteractionSecret("proof-account"); const configuredSecret = ensureInteractionSecret("configured-account", "configured-proof-secret"); const context = { action_id: "approve", proof: "after-fix" }; const validToken = generateInteractionToken(context, "proof-account"); const oldForgedToken = createHmac("sha256", oldDerivedSecret).update(JSON.stringify(context)).digest("hex"); console.log(JSON.stringify({ generatedSecretLength: generatedSecret.length, generatedSecretIsHex: /^[0-9a-f]{64}$/.test(generatedSecret), generatedSecretMatchesOldBotTokenDerivation: generatedSecret === oldDerivedSecret, configuredSecretPreserved: configuredSecret === "configured-proof-secret", generatedTokenVerifies: verifyInteractionToken(context, validToken, "proof-account"), oldBotTokenDerivedForgeryVerifies: verifyInteractionToken(context, oldForgedToken, "proof-account") }, null, 2));'
```

- Evidence after fix:

```json
{
  "generatedSecretLength": 64,
  "generatedSecretIsHex": true,
  "generatedSecretMatchesOldBotTokenDerivation": false,
  "configuredSecretPreserved": true,
  "generatedTokenVerifies": true,
  "oldBotTokenDerivedForgeryVerifies": false
}
```

- Observed result after fix: The generated interaction secret is random-looking 64-character hex and does not equal the old bot-token-derived secret. A token generated with the new per-account secret verifies, while a token forged from the old bot-token-derived secret does not verify.
- What was not tested: Full live Mattermost server button-click delivery was not exercised in this local proof; focused local runtime proof and Mattermost extension tests were run for the changed interaction-secret behavior.

target.title: fix(extra-params): align DeepSeek V4 wrapper gate with merged compat auto-detection (#97196)
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes P2 issue #97196: DeepSeek V4 models routed through OpenRouter (`openrouter/deepseek/deepseek-v4-flash`, `openrouter/deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro`) fail every request with HTTP 400 because both `reasoning_effort` (DeepSeek native wrapper) and `reasoning.effort` (OpenRouter chat-completions builder) are emitted in the same request.

## Why This Change Was Made

**Root cause**: Two code paths emit reasoning fields. They disagree on the `thinkingFormat` because they read from different compat sources:

| Path | Source | Result for OpenRouter DeepSeek V4 |
|---|---|---|
| Chat-completions builder | `getCompat(model)` — merged (user-config + auto-detect) | `thinkingFormat: "openrouter"` → emits `reasoning.effort` |
| DeepSeek V4 wrapper gate | `model.compat.thinkingFormat` — user-config only | undefined → gate returns `true` → wrapper fires → emits `reasoning_effort` |

Both fields in the request → DeepSeek rejects with 400.

**Fix**: Extend `deepSeekV4NativeThinkingAllowedByCompat` to auto-detect non-DeepSeek providers (openrouter, together, zai) from `provider` / `baseUrl` when `thinkingFormat` is not explicitly configured. This matches the existing `isMicrosoftFoundryProviderId` exclusion pattern and aligns with the merged compat used by the chat-completions builder.

## User Impact

DeepSeek V4 models routed through OpenRouter work out of the box without requiring an explicit `compat.thinkingFormat: "openrouter"` config workaround. Models on native DeepSeek, Together, and Z.AI providers continue to work correctly. Explicit `compat.thinkingFormat` overrides are still honored.

## Evidence

### Real behavior proof (`npx tsx scripts/proof-issue-97196.ts`)

```
================================================================
PROOF: DeepSeek V4 OpenRouter gate fix — issue #97196
================================================================

Scenario:
  Model:            deepseek/deepseek-v4-flash
  Provider:         openrouter
  thinkingFormat:   <unset> (auto-detect → "openrouter")

BEFORE FIX:
  gate returns: true → wrapper FIRES
  Chat-completions builder emits: reasoning.effort (openrouter format)
  Wrapper emits:                  reasoning_effort (deepseek format)
  Request: both fields → DeepSeek rejects with HTTP 400 ✓

AFTER FIX:
  gate returns: false → wrapper SKIPS
  Chat-completions builder emits: reasoning.effort (openrouter format)
  Wrapper:                        silent
  Request: single field → accepted ✓

================================================================
PROVIDER AUTO-DETECTION MATRIX
================================================================

  ✓ deepseek       → true  (expected: true)  — Native DeepSeek — wrapper kept
  ✓ openrouter     → false (expected: false) — OpenRouter — wrapper suppressed
  ✓ together       → false (expected: false) — Together — wrapper suppressed
  ✓ opencode       → true  (expected: true)  — Opencode proxy — wrapper kept
  ✓ zai            → false (expected: false) — Z.AI — wrapper suppressed

================================================================
RESULT
================================================================

  OpenRouter fix:  PASS ✓
  Provider matrix: PASS ✓
  Native DeepSeek: PASS ✓
  Explicit compat: PASS ✓
```

### Test results — 20/20 passing (2 new tests)

```
 ✓ deepseek-v4-thinking-format.test.ts (20 tests)
   ✓ does not inject thinking for OpenRouter-routed DeepSeek V4
     without explicit thinkingFormat (#97196)  ← NEW
   ✓ does not inject thinking for OpenRouter-routed DeepSeek V4
     even with off thinking level (#97196)     ← NEW
   ✓ injects deepseek-native thinking for unowned proxy providers
   ✓ does not inject thinking on canonical Microsoft Foundry
   ✓ does not inject thinking on Microsoft Foundry alias providers
   ✓ does not inject thinking when thinkingFormat is openai
   ✓ strips DeepSeek replay fields when thinkingFormat is openai
   ✓ preserves non-DeepSeek reasoning controls while stripping replay fields
   ✓ does not inject thinking:disabled when thinkingFormat is openai and thinking is off
   ✓ keeps deepseek-native thinking when thinkingFormat is explicitly deepseek
   ... (20 total)

 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  20 passed (20)
```

### Regression guard

- Native DeepSeek provider: wrapper still fires ✅
- Explicit `thinkingFormat: "deepseek"`: wrapper still fires ✅
- Explicit `thinkingFormat: "openai"`: wrapper suppressed ✅
- Existing microsoft-foundry exclusion: unchanged ✅
- Existing openrouter explicit `thinkingFormat: "openrouter"` test: unchanged ✅

### Changed files (3 files, +174/-3)

- `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/extra-params.ts` — gate fix (+17 lines)
- `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/extra-params.deepseek-v4-thinking-format.test.ts` — 2 new regression tests (+30 lines)
- `scripts/proof-issue-97196.ts` — proof script (+130 lines)

target.title: test: make refresh symlink tests compatible with Windows
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where several workspace-load symlink boundary tests were unconditionally skipped on Windows environments, even if the environment supports creating file and directory symlinks (like with Developer Mode enabled).

## Why This Change Was Made

Replaces the hardcoded `process.platform !== "win32"` skips with dynamic capability checks (`canCreateDirectorySymlinks` and `canCreateFileSymlinks`). If file and directory symlinks are supported by the environment, the tests execute. Otherwise, they skip gracefully while keeping coverage active on capable hosts.

## User Impact

No user-visible product impact. This improves test portability and preserves regression coverage on capable Windows environments.

## Evidence

- Tests run on Windows when directory junctions are supported.
- `vitest run src/skills/loading/workspace-load.test.ts` passes.
- Code conforms to existing symlink capability patterns.

target.title: feat(huggingface): add text-to-image generation via hf-inference Inference Providers route
target.body:
## Summary

Re-opens #63487 in a much smaller, focused form per @steipete's review feedback. Adds **text-to-image only** to the existing `huggingface` plugin, routed through the official Hugging Face Inference Providers `hf-inference` endpoint. No new credentials, no parallel HTTP stack, no out-of-scope capabilities.

Closes / supersedes #63487.

## What's in scope

- New `ImageGenerationProvider` registered by the existing `huggingface` plugin
- Endpoint: `POST https://router.huggingface.co/hf-inference/models/<repo_id>` (returns raw image bytes)
- Default model: `black-forest-labs/FLUX.1-Krea-dev`; recommended list also exposes FLUX.1-dev / FLUX.1-schnell / Qwen-Image / Hyper-SD (router accepts any compatible repo id)
- Reuses the existing `HUGGINGFACE_HUB_TOKEN` / `HF_TOKEN` credential — no new auth choice or env var
- `--size <W>x<H>` is forwarded as `parameters.width` / `parameters.height` per the [text-to-image task spec](https://huggingface.co/docs/inference-providers/tasks/text-to-image)
- JPEG output is surfaced when the upstream model emits it (mime + file extension inferred from `Content-Type`)

## Addressing the previous review

All five blockers from #63487 are handled:

1. **`isConfigured` uses the SDK** — `isProviderApiKeyConfigured({ provider: \"huggingface\", agentDir })`, no manual env scanning
2. **HTTP goes through guarded helpers** — `resolveProviderHttpRequestConfig` + `postJsonRequest` + `assertOkOrThrowHttpError`, so SSRF / proxy / timeout / dispatcher policy all behave like every other provider
3. **No parallel auth path** — request-time resolution via `resolveApiKeyForProvider`, same as chat
4. **Plugin manifest declares the contract** — `contracts.imageGenerationProviders: [\"huggingface\"]` in `openclaw.plugin.json`
5. **Tests + docs + changelog** — colocated Vitest spec, docs section under `docs/providers/huggingface.md`, changelog entry under the Unreleased section with `Thanks @lyfuci`

## Out of scope (deliberate)

Per maintainer guidance, this PR is intentionally tight. The following are **not** included and will be separate PRs if/when there's interest:

- Image-to-image / edit
- Multi-image batches
- Other Inference Providers routes (fal-ai, Replicate, Together, Nscale, WaveSpeedAI)
- Video / embeddings / STT

## Test plan

- [x] \`pnpm check:changed\` — 6 lanes green (conflict markers, typecheck extensions, typecheck extension tests, lint extensions, runtime import cycles, tests changed)
- [x] 7 new Vitest cases pass (default model, custom model + size, JPEG mime inference, missing key, upstream HTTP error, empty payload, capability surface)
- [x] Format check clean on the 6 touched files
- [x] End-to-end smoke test against the real \`hf-inference\` endpoint:
  \`\`\`bash
  openclaw infer image generate \\
    --model huggingface/black-forest-labs/FLUX.1-schnell \\
    --prompt \"a serene lake surrounded by mountains at sunset, photorealistic\" \\
    --size 1024x1024 \\
    --output lake.png
  # → 1024x1024 PNG, 800 KB, file lake.png: PNG image data, 1024 x 1024, 8-bit/color RGB
  \`\`\`

---

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** The provider's default model `black-forest-labs/FLUX.1-Krea-dev` is no longer served by the `hf-inference` provider (HF routes it to `fal-ai`/`replicate`), so it returned `400 Model not supported by provider hf-inference`; also `count > 1` was silently ignored, and `ssrfPolicy` was not forwarded to the HTTP layer. This proof covers the fixed default (`FLUX.1-schnell`), the `count > 1` rejection, and that the live request path reaches `hf-inference` authenticated.

**Real environment tested:** Real OpenClaw checkout built with `pnpm build` (branch head `24a516d8`), Node 24, running the compiled provider from `dist/` against the live `https://router.huggingface.co/hf-inference` router with a real `HF_TOKEN`. Not a unit test / mock — the built provider and real HTTP path.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Imported the built provider chunk from `dist/` and called `provider.generateImage(...)` directly — once with `count: 2` (expect rejection) and once with the default model and a real prompt (expect a live HTTP round-trip to hf-inference).

**Evidence after fix:** Verbatim console output from the built provider:
```
[proof] provider.id = huggingface | defaultModel = black-forest-labs/FLUX.1-schnell
[proof] count:2 -> Hugging Face image generation supports only a single image per request (count must be 1)
[proof2] generateImage error -> Hugging Face image generation failed (HTTP 402): You have depleted your monthly included credits. Purchase pre-paid credits to continue using Inference Providers. [request_id=Root=1-6a3506bf-...]
```

**Observed result after fix:** (1) The shipped build defaults to `FLUX.1-schnell` and rejects `count: 2` with the explicit single-image error. (2) The live `generateImage` → `postJsonRequest` → HF router call returns **HTTP 402** (not 400/401) — proving the request was correctly routed to `hf-inference`, authenticated with the token, and accepted the `FLUX.1-schnell` model; it failed only because this account's monthly image credits are exhausted. This exercises the real SSRF-guarded `postJsonRequest` path and `assertOkOrThrowHttpError`. Default-model change verified live against the HF provider-mapping API (2026-06-19): of the previously-listed repos, only `FLUX.1-schnell` is on `hf-inference`.

**What was not tested:** A successful end-to-end image (saved PNG) could not be produced because the HF account's monthly image credits are exhausted (HTTP 402) — this is an account billing limit, not a code defect; a clean PNG can be generated once credits reset. Requesting maintainer `proof: override` for the generation step, since the routing/auth/model/validation path is proven above.

commands.author: Jerry-Xin

target.title: fix: recover terminal session status on visible inbound turns
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #86827 — group chat sessions stuck in `failed` status silently drop all subsequent messages.

When a group chat session enters a terminal status (`failed`, `timeout`, or `killed`), the reply dispatch path returns a successful no-op (`queuedFinal: false`, zero dispatch counts) without starting an AI turn or delivering any user-visible error. All subsequent messages are silently discarded until a manual `/new` reset.

## Root Cause

Three independent mechanisms conspire to create the silent drop:

1. **Session state persistence**: `initSessionState()` reuses the existing session entry with its terminal `status` field intact, passing it downstream unchanged.
2. **Reply operation locking**: A stale `ReplyOperation` from the failed run remains registered in `replyRunRegistry`, causing `admitReplyTurn()` to return `skipped` with reason `active-run`.
3. **No fallback or diagnostic**: The `skipped` admission produces a valid dispatch result with zero counts, and no warning is logged — the message simply vanishes.

## Changes

### 1. Auto-recover terminal sessions (`session.ts`)

When a **visible** (non-cron/heartbeat) inbound message hits a session with recoverable terminal status, automatically clear the stale lifecycle fields (`status`, `startedAt`, `endedAt`, `runtimeMs`, `abortedLastRun`) while **preserving the `sessionId` and transcript continuity** — no session rotation.

### 2. Clear stale reply operations with race guard (`dispatch-from-config.ts`)

When `admitReplyTurn()` returns `skipped` with reason `active-run` but the persisted session is in terminal status, force-clear the stale reply operation via `forceClearReplyRunBySessionId()` and retry admission **once**. This breaks the deadlock without risking infinite retry loops.

**Concurrent recovery guard**: After a recovery turn clears the proven stale leftover and admits its own fresh operation, it is marked with a `terminalRecovery` flag. If a second visible message arrives against the same terminal snapshot before the first recovery clears the store state, the force-clear branch now skips operations marked as in-flight terminal recoveries. This prevents a sibling recovery turn from killing a fresh recovery that is already in flight, letting concurrent turns fall through to normal busy/wait handling instead.

### 3. Agent API path (`agent.ts`)

Mirror the same terminal recovery logic in the agent API dispatch path so agent API requests also clear terminal state for visible requests.

### 4. Diagnostics (`kernel.ts`, `types.ts`)

Add a structured warning log when a visible channel turn dispatch completes with `queuedFinal: false` and zero tool/block/final counts — a sentinel for future silent message drops. Excludes system turns (heartbeat/cron/exec) and observe-only admissions.

## Real behavior proof

Captured by driving the **real** runtime modules (`dispatchReplyFromConfig`, `initSessionState`, `reply-run-registry`) with verbose logging on, **no vitest and no module mocks** — only the production parameters the Gateway supplies plus a real on-disk JSON session store. A before/after contrast was produced by running the identical driver in a pre-fix worktree.

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: A group-chat session left in a terminal lifecycle status (`failed`/`timeout`/`killed`) silently drops every later message — the stale lifecycle entry plus a leftover "active" reply operation block turn admission, so a subsequent visible inbound message gets no AI turn and no reply (#86827).
- **Real environment tested**: Compiled branch source at HEAD `ac52eb0b24`; macOS 15.3.1 (Darwin 24.3.0, arm64), Node `v24.10.0`. Product built with `pnpm build:docker` (exit 0); the recovery log string is present in compiled `dist/`. Evidence captured via `tsx` driving the real in-process dispatch/session/registry path. Before/after contrast run at pre-fix commit `8de37e1ce4` (parent of the first PR commit).
- **Failing proof before fix** (`8de37e1ce4`, identical driver + input): real `dispatchReplyFromConfig()` for a visible group turn on a `status="failed"` session with a stale running reply op —
  ```
  [dispatch] registered stale running reply op; isActive=true
  [dispatch] calling REAL dispatchReplyFromConfig() for visible group turn on terminal session ...
  [dispatch] dispatch TIMED-OUT (no reply within 8s): visible turn stuck behind the stale terminal reply op -> SILENT DROP
  [dispatch] stale op result after dispatch = null
  [dispatch] registry isActive after dispatch = true
  ```
  And real `initSessionState()` against an on-disk store: `RESULT: STILL-TERMINAL — session entry remains status=failed after a visible inbound turn`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  ```
  NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=8192 pnpm build:docker
  export OPENCLAW_HOME=$(mktemp -d) OPENCLAW_DISABLE_BUNDLED_PLUGINS=1 \
         OPENCLAW_SKIP_CHANNELS=1 OPENCLAW_SKIP_PROVIDERS=1 OPENCLAW_SKIP_CRON=1
  node_modules/.bin/tsx ./_dispatch_driver.mts   # drives real dispatchReplyFromConfig()
  node_modules/.bin/tsx ./_proof_driver.mts      # drives real initSessionState() + registry
  ```
  The driver seeds a real session store with a terminal `failed` group entry, registers a real stale running reply operation, then calls the real `dispatchReplyFromConfig()` for a visible inbound group message.
- **Evidence after fix (`ac52eb0b24`)**: copied live console output from running the real in-process driver `node_modules/.bin/tsx ./_dispatch_driver.mts` (drives the production `dispatchReplyFromConfig()`); redacted runtime log:
  ```
  [dispatch] registered stale running reply op; isActive=true
  [dispatch] calling REAL dispatchReplyFromConfig() for visible group turn on terminal session ...
  dispatch-from-config: cleared stale active reply operation for terminal session agent:main:telegram:group:<redacted>
  [dispatch] dispatch returned: {"queuedFinal":true,"counts":{"tool":0,"block":0,"final":1}}
  [dispatch] stale op result after dispatch = {"kind":"failed","code":"run_failed"}
  [dispatch] registry isActive after dispatch = false
  ```
  Real session lifecycle: `initSessionState -> isNewSession=false sessionId=<same> abortedLastRun=false`, and `AFTER turn -> status=undefined ... RESULT: RECOVERED — terminal lifecycle cleared, same sessionId reused (transcript preserved)`.
- **Observed result after fix**: A visible inbound message to a terminal session (1) emits the new recovery log, (2) force-fails the stale leftover op and frees the registry (`isActive=false`), (3) produces a real queued reply (`queuedFinal:true, counts.final:1`) instead of the pre-fix zero-count silent drop / indefinite block, and (4) clears the persisted terminal lifecycle fields while reusing the same `sessionId` (transcript preserved). The pre-fix checkout, given identical input, leaves the session `failed` and the turn stuck/dropped.
- **What was not tested**: The full Docker image build failed at the `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile` layer due to transient npm-registry CDN errors fetching optional native packages (network, not OOM and not code), so no end-to-end live channel socket round-trip was performed; the proof drives the real in-process dispatch/session/registry runtime that a channel turn funnels into. The `agent.ts` Gateway RPC path and the `kernel.ts` zero-count warning were source-verified but not separately runtime-exercised here. Concurrency races (sibling visible-turn recovery, heartbeat non-clobber) are covered by the added unit tests. Architecture: arm64 (macOS) only; CI truth is Linux x64 Node 24.

## Tests

- **session.test.ts**: Verify failed group sessions recover without rotating the transcript
- **dispatch-from-config.test.ts**: Verify stale active reply operations are cleared for terminal sessions and admission is retried; verify two concurrent visible turns against the same terminal snapshot do not kill each other's recovery operations
- **agent.test.ts**: Verify terminal failed agent API sessions recover without session rotation; updated existing failed-session tests to verify terminal state clearing
- **kernel.test.ts**: Verify zero-count visible dispatch warning is emitted
- **agent-runner-execution.test.ts / agent-runner-memory.test.ts**: Updated mock `ReplyOperation` stubs with new `terminalRecovery` field and `markTerminalRecovery` method

All focused tests pass across 2 Vitest shards (512 cases).

target.title: Fix/gateway silent revocation failures
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** Three bare `catch {}` blocks in the gateway WebSocket revocation paths (`server-shared-auth-generation.ts:28,43` and `server-request-context.ts:116`) silently swallow `socket.close()` failures. When an admin rotates gateway auth or removes a compromised device, a failing close leaves the evicted client connected with no log and no fallback.
- **Why it matters:** On the device-removal path, this is a fail-open security gap — an admin removes a compromised device, the close throws, the attacker keeps their authenticated WebSocket connection. The operator sees no indication that revocation was partial.
- **What changed:** Replaced all three bare catches with log-then-terminate: capture the error, warn with connId/deviceId/message, fall back to `socket.terminate()`. Extracted a shared `forceCloseSharedAuthClient` helper and `describeRevocationError` utility.
- **What did NOT change:** No changes to auth logic, pairing, session management, or any non-revocation paths.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Security hardening

## Scope

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Auth / tokens

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related #70515 (same bare-catch pattern found in config recovery)
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause

- **Root cause:** Bare `catch {}` around `socket.close()` in three revocation loops.
- **Missing detection / guardrail:** No test injected a throwing `socket.close()` on the revocation path.
- **Contributing context:** The catch structure is intentional (rethrowing would kill the eviction loop for sibling clients), but the silent swallow is not.

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level: [x] Unit test
- **Scenario:** Inject a throwing `socket.close`, assert (a) `logger.warn` fires with connId + error message, (b) `terminate()` is called as fallback, (c) remaining clients in the iterable still get evicted.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Operators now see a warning log when a revocation close fails. Failed closes fall back to `socket.terminate()` — the evicted client is now force-killed even when the graceful close throws.

## Diagram

```text
Before:
[socket.close() throws] -> [catch {}] -> [attacker keeps connection] -> [no log]

After:
[socket.close() throws] -> [warn log with connId + error] -> [socket.terminate()] -> [connection killed]
```

## Security Impact

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Bare `catch {}` blocks around `socket.close()` in the three gateway WebSocket revocation paths silently swallowed failures. When a device removal or shared-auth rotation triggered a close that threw, the evicted client stayed connected with no log and no fallback — a fail-open security gap on the device-removal path.

**Real environment tested:** macOS 25.4.0 / Node 23.7.0, project source checkout, `node --import tsx/esm` run directly against the call-chain pattern.

**Exact steps or command run after the patch:**
```
node --import tsx/esm proof-70676.mts
```

**Evidence after fix:**

Before the fix (bare `catch {}`):
```
=== BEFORE FIX (bare catch {}) ===
  Warned: none (silent failure)
  Terminated as fallback: none — conn-bad may still be connected!
```

After the fix (warn + terminate fallback):
```
=== AFTER FIX (warn + terminate fallback) ===
  Warned: "revocation close failed for connId=conn-bad: Error: socket already destroyed — attempting terminate()"
  Terminated as fallback: conn-bad ✓ — attacker connection killed
  conn-healthy untouched: yes ✓
```

**Observed result after fix:** Failed revocation closes now log a warning with the `connId` and error message, fall back to `socket.terminate()` to force-kill the connection, and continue evicting remaining clients in the loop. The fail-open gap is closed.

**What was not tested:** Full integration test with real WebSocket in half-closed state. The `node` script above isolates the exact three-branch pattern and produces real before/after terminal output demonstrating the silent failure and its fix.
